segments.s629-g9: This is a verbless/perfective description: برآورده‌ای is a past participle/adjectival form meaning ‘raised’ (here used predicatively) followed by بود as an auxiliary in the past, so the clause means ‘(his) head was raised.’ The word order places the state (برآورده‌ای) before the copula (بود) and the locative phrase سر در هوا follows to indicate what was raised.
segments.s629-g10: This is an adverbial/locative phrase describing the surroundings: بدربر (here ‘around/about’) qualifies the following nominal group; فراوان is an adjective modifying the following nouns سلیح و نوا (‘arms and music’). The coordination سلیح و نوا lists two types of things present ‘in abundance.’
segments.s629-g11: This lists the agents: سواران و پیلان is a coordinated subject (‘horsemen and elephants’). بدربر again marks ‘around/about’ and به پای here introduces the locus/temporal framing (‘at the feet / before’ or ‘to the sound of’ depending on idiom). Word order places the noun phrase before the following verbal phrase.
segments.s629-g12: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive meaning ‘to roar’ or ‘roaring’ describing the sound/action these beings made; زنگ with کرنای (with trumpet) specifies the kind of sound — ‘the alarm/ringing with the horn.’ The preposition با links the means/instrument (کرنای) to the sound (زنگ).
segments.s629-g13: This clause uses an adjectival/participial notion (شگفتی, ‘astonishment’) applied to بان بارگه (‘the court‑keeper’), with the verb بماند (‘remained/stood’) at the end as the main predicate. The preposition بر here accompanies بماند in an idiomatic locative/continuative sense: ‘stood/was there in astonishment.’
segments.s629-g14: This is a causative/resultive expression: دلش را (his heart, marked by the object particle را) is the thing affected; به اندیشه اندر is a prepositional phrase ‘into thought/into pondering’; نشاند is an archaic past verb meaning ‘placed/settled’ (here ‘he set his heart to thinking’). The sequence shows object+prepositional goal+past verb.
segments.s629-g15: چنین is an adverb (‘thus/so’) placed before the verb; گفت is the past tense verb ‘said/spoke’ (here used transitively), and با پرده‌داران اوی is the indirect object/recipient (‘to/with his curtain‑keepers’). The possessive اوی attaches to پرده‌داران and indicates ‘his chamberlains.’
segments.s629-g16: This is a nominal apposition/coordinated list describing those addressed: پرستنده و پای‌کاران are two coordinated nouns (‘devotees/adorers and steadfast workers/companions’), and اوی is the possessive ‘his,’ applying to both nouns. The whole functions as the addressee or descriptive apposition.
segments.s629-g1: چو is the classical subordinating particle 'when'. بنهاد is the past (perfect) form of نهادن (to put/place) with the old prefix ب-; the construction بر + نامه‌بر مهر شاه means 'upon the messenger (نامه‌بر) the king's مهر (seal)' — مهر شاه is a genitival noun phrase 'the king's seal'. The whole clause = 'when he put the king's seal upon the messenger/entrusted the envoy with the king's seal.'
segments.s629-g2: برآراست is the past of آراستن with a prefixed بر- meaning 'adorned/arrayed'. بر ساز is a classical, compact way to indicate accompaniment ('to the sound of / with instruments'); ساز here means musical instrument/tune. نخچیرگاه is the place modified by this action (the hunting-ground). So the clause says he 'adorned the hunting-ground with instruments/music.'
segments.s629-g3: به لشکر is a locative/partitive phrase 'among/for the host/army'. ز کارش literally 'of/from his affair' (ز used poetically = 'about/of'), and کس آگه نبود is a negative past 'no one was aware'. Word order is typical classical Persian: prepositional phrase → genitive phrase → subject complement → نبود (negative copula).
segments.s629-g4: جز از introduces an exception 'except from/of'. نامدارانش is the possessive plural 'his famed/notable men' (ش is the possessive suffix). همره نبود is 'there was no companion/none accompanied (him)'; here همره functions as predicate 'companion' and نبود negates it. Together: 'except for his famed men, no companions were with him.'
segments.s629-g5: بیامد is simple past 'he came'. بدین‌سان is an adverb 'in this manner/thus' modifying the verb. به هندوستان is the destination 'to India/Hindustan' (به as directional preposition). The clause = 'He thus came to Hindustan.'
segments.s629-g6: گذشت is past of گذشتن 'he passed/crossed'. The poetic prepositional bundle از بر آب (از + بر) here denotes motion across or over the water. جادوستان is a place-name 'the land/place of magic' functioning as the object/location crossed. So: 'he crossed over the water of Jādūstān.'
segments.s629-g7: چو 'when' introduces the temporal clause. نزدیک ایوان شنگل is 'near the iwan (portico) of Shangal' (نزدیک as adverb/adjective + noun phrase). رسید is the past 'arrived/reached/came near'. The clause means 'When he came near Shangal's iwan.'
segments.s629-g8: در پرده و بارگاهش بدید: در پرده 'within/inside the curtain' (در = in), و links 'and' to بارگاهش ('its court/palace' with the possessive -ش), and بدید is the classical past form of دیدن ('he saw'). The verb applies to the whole coordinate phrase: 'he saw (its) curtains and palace.'
segments.s629-g25: This clause names two contrasting materials applied to the man: "ازارش" (an item of his dress/ornament — here functioning as a nominal modifier of appearance) + "همه سیم" (literally “all silver”) and "پیکرش زر" ("his body/figure [is] gold"). Word order is poetic: adjectives/materials follow the nouns they modify or appear in apposition; the copula is omitted (as is normal in Persian), so the materials are understood predicatively.
segments.s629-g26: "نشانده" is a past participle (placed/settled) used adjectivally/predicatively; it governs the locative phrase "به هر جای" (in/at every place). "چندی گهر" means "several gems" or "gems here and there" — "چندی" quantifies "گهر." The whole line means jewels have been set in various places on him; the preposition "به" marks the locative/target.
segments.s629-g27: "نشسته" is a stative past participle meaning "seated." The locative expression "به نزدیک او" (= "near him") precedes the noun "رهنمای" (guide/attendant), so literally "seated near him, an attendant." This inverted order (participle + locative + referent) is common in poetic Persian.
segments.s629-g28: "پس پشت او" is a compound locative meaning "at his back/behind him." "ایستاده" is another stative participle "standing." "به پای" (= "at the foot/at his feet") further specifies location. Together they describe someone standing behind him, at his feet; prepositions follow the participle in this poetic compression.
segments.s629-g29: "برادرش را دید" is straightforward: "he saw his brother," where "را" is the definite/direct-object marker attached to "برادرش." The following locative "بر زیرگاه" ("on/at the footstool/low place") specifies where the brother was seen; the verbal action is past simple "دید."
segments.s629-g30: "نهاده" is a perfect/past participle meaning "placed/put (on)." The phrase "نهاده به سر بر ز گوهر کلاه" is compressed: "نهاده به سر" = "placed on the head," and "بر ز گوهر کلاه" = "a cap made of/with gems" (poetic "ز" = "from/of"); the preposition "بر" here marks the surface "on" and the genitive/material "ز گوهر" describes the cap.
segments.s629-g31: "چو آمد" is an archaic/poetic temporal clause meaning "when he came." "به نزدیک" marks approach "near/into proximity." "شنگل فراز" is a fused place-name or descriptive phrase ("shangal" + "fraz/faraz" = "the high/shangol summit/pavilion"); the noun phrase follows the locative as often in verse.
segments.s629-g32: "ورا دید" = "he saw him/thereupon he saw (him/it)," where "ورا" is a demonstrative/pronominal adverb meaning "to him/there." "با تاج بر تخت ناز" is a prepositional cluster: "با تاج" (with a crown) + "بر تخت ناز" (on a throne of nâz); "بر" marks the surface/location, and "ناز" is a poetic quality of the throne ("grace/defensive pride/splendor").
segments.s629-g33: A noun phrase: 'همه' (quantifier) modifies the head noun 'پایه' (the legs/pedestals). 'تخت' follows to show possession ('the throne's pedestals'), and 'زر و بلور' are material/ornamental qualifiers placed after the head noun (gold and crystal). Word order is typical literary Persian: quantifier + head noun + possessor + postmodifiers.
segments.s629-g34: 'نشسته' is a past participle meaning 'seated' and here heads the verbal phrase. 'برو' is a fused older form = بر + او ('on it'), referring back to the throne (this particular spelling represents the preposition plus pronoun); it functions as a locative 'on it' rather than the modern verb 'to go' (no matching sense in the provided list). The subject 'شاه' appears after the participle (inverted order): 'the king sat on it'. 'با فر و زور' is an instrumental/attendant phrase 'with glory and might'.
segments.s629-g35: 'بر تخت شد شاه' literally 'became on the throne the king' — here 'شد' (past) means 'mounted / became seated on'. 'بر' + 'تخت' is the locative 'upon the throne' and word order is literary (verb before subject). The coordinated clause 'و بردش نماز' uses 'بردش' (برد + enclitic ش = 'took/brought him' or 'paid to him') with 'نماز' meaning ritual obeisance/homage; the clause states that homage/prayer was offered to him. (The lexical senses given for 'برد' in the prompt do not include this verbal sense, so it is treated as the verb 'to give/take' with clitic object.)
segments.s629-g36: 'همی بود' is a literary/periphrastic past sense meaning 'it was / it remained'. 'پیشش' is locative 'in his presence' or 'before him'. 'زمانی دراز' is a temporal phrase 'for a long time' (زمانی = for a time / for a while, دراز = long). Together: 'it remained before him for a long time.'
segments.s629-g37: 'چنین گفت' is a fixed literary phrase 'thus he said / thus spoke.' The following relative construction 'زان کو' is archaic: 'زان' = 'that/from that' and 'کو' functions as a relative 'who' (the provided senses did not include this relative use). 'ز شاهان' is a genitive 'of the kings' and 'مهست' is a predicative adjective 'is moonlike / is as the moon' (a poetic way to say 'most beautiful/splendid among kings'). Word order is inverted and compact as in classical verse.
segments.s629-g38: A nominal apposition/identification: 'جهاندار' (sovereign, world-holder) followed by the name 'بهرام' and the epithet 'یزدان‌پرست' (God-worshipper). This lists titles/names without a verb — typical in epic naming.
segments.s629-g39: 'یکی' ('a certain') introduces an indefinite item: 'a certain letter'. 'نامه دارم' = 'I have a letter' (دارم present tense 'I have'). 'بر شاه هند' uses 'بر' to mark recipient/target 'for/to the king of Hind (India)'. The clause is subject (implicit 'I') + object + verb typical of Persian.
segments.s629-g40: 'نوشته خطی پهلوی' is a noun phrase describing the letter: 'نوشته' here = 'written' (a written text), 'خطی' relates to script/hand (i.e., 'in script / script-related'), and 'پهلوی' names the language/script (Pahlavi). 'بر پرند' (= 'on a bird') uses 'بر' as locative 'on' and 'پرند' = 'bird' — i.e., the letter was written in Pahlavi script on a bird (a carrier pigeon or the like).
segments.s629-g17: 'که' is the relative/complementizer 'that/who' introducing this clause. 'از نزد' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'from the presence of' (نزد = 'in the presence of'). 'پیروز' is an epithet 'the Victorious' modifying the following title-name, and 'بهرامشاه' is the compound title 'King Bahram'. Word order is preposition + noun phrase indicating source ('from the presence of ...').
segments.s629-g18: 'فرستاده آمد' literally 'a messenger came' (فرستاده here reads as 'messenger' / a sent one rather than a verbal passive). 'بدین بارگاه' = به + این + بارگاه, i.e. 'to this court/palace' (orthographically shown as ب + دین in the segmentation; functionally it is the preposition 'به' + demonstrative 'این'). The verb آمد is simple past 'came'.
segments.s629-g19: 'هم اندر زمان' is an adverbial phrase 'at the same time' or 'in that very moment' (هم = also/at the same time, اندر زمان = in the time/moment). 'رفت' is the simple past 'he went'. 'سالار بار' is a compound title 'the chief (سالار) of the load/baggage (بار)' — i.e. the officer in charge of the baggage. The phrase places the time before the verb.
segments.s629-g20: 'ز پرده درون' = 'from (behind) the curtain within' (ز = from; پرده = curtain; درون = inside/within). 'تا' here marks motion or extent 'as far as/to' (older usage 'up to, until, toward'), and 'بر شهریار' means 'toward the king' (بر = toward/to; شهریار = king). Together they describe carrying the load from inside the curtain up to the king's presence.
segments.s629-g21: 'بفرمود' is an honorific/simple past meaning 'he ordered/said' (polite past used by the poet). 'تا' here functions as a connective meaning 'so/and then' indicating consequence, and 'پرده برداشتند' is a past-plural clause 'they removed/lifted the curtain' (برداشتند = they took/removed).
segments.s629-g22: 'به ارجش' literally 'to/with his honor' — ارجش is ارج (honor/value) with the clitic -ش and indicates that the placing was done with honor. 'ز درگاه' here situates the action in the royal presence/court (ز as poetic preposition, درگاه = court/royal presence). 'بگذاشتند' is past plural 'they placed/put' (object 'him' is implied). The phrase as a whole means they respectfully took him into the royal presence and placed him there.
segments.s629-g23: 'خرامان' is an adverb 'with a proud/stately gait'. 'همی' is the old progressive/continuative particle (here giving a vivid continuous past, often translated as 'strode/was striding'). 'رفت' is the past 'he went/strided'. 'بهرام گور' is the subject (proper name with epithet); note the subject comes after the verb for poetic inversion. The epithet 'گور' here functions as part of the name (traditionally 'Gūr' = onager/epithet).
segments.s629-g24: 'یکی' is the indefinite 'a certain' (introducing some particular but unspecified thing). 'خانه دید' = 'he saw a house' (دید = saw; the object 'خانه' follows the particle). 'آسمانش بلور' describes that house: 'its sky/ceiling was crystal' (آسمانش = its sky/heaven with possessive clitic -ش; بلور = crystal). In Persian a relative description like 'whose ... was ...' is expressed by juxtaposing the possessed phrase after the noun as here.
segments.s629-g41: چو introduces a temporal subordinate clause 'when'. آواز بهرام is a genitive/possessive phrase 'Bahram's voice' (noun + name). بشنید is simple past 3rd singular 'he heard'; شاه is the subject placed after the verb in this poetic word order (common in Ferdowsi).
segments.s629-g42: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past 'he commanded/ordered'. زرین is an adjective 'golden' modifying the following noun phrase. یکی here functions as an indefinite determiner 'a certain / a' (introducing one item). زیرگاه is a noun 'low seat / low station' — together they form the object of بفرمود (no explicit را is used).
segments.s629-g43: بران is an adverbial locative 'upon that / thereon', referring back to the newly ordered seat. کرسی زرش is a noun phrase 'a throne/seat of gold' (زرش as a gold-marking/ adjectival idea). بنشاندند is past plural 'they seated / they placed' (causative sense: they set/installed).
segments.s629-g44: ز is the poetic preposition 'from', here introducing source/place. درگاه means 'court / royal presence'. یارانش (یاران + ش) is 'his companions' with the possessive suffix ش. را is the definite object marker attached to یارانش, and خواندند is past plural 'they called / they summoned' (here 'summoned' fits the context).
segments.s629-g45: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. بنشست is past 3rd singular 'he sat down'. بگشاد here functions as past action 'he opened / loosened' (the word's sense given is 'opening/relieving'), and لب را z بند is an idiomatic object: 'he opened his lips (را marks the object) from the bond' meaning he freed his speech from restraint.
segments.s629-g46: چنین 'thus' signals the manner of speech. گفت functions here as the main past verb 'he said' (the provided lemma senses do not list the verbal sense, so the exact sense index is not available). کای is a vocative particle 'O' used to address royalty; شهریار is 'king' and بلند is an honorific adjective 'lofty / exalted' addressing the addressee.
segments.s629-g47: زبان here means 'speech / utterance' (not merely the organ). برگشایم is first‑person verb 'I shall open / I will unfold' (present/subjunctive form used for a future/conditional sense). چو فرمان دهی is a temporal/conditional clause 'when you give the order' (دهی is 2nd person present/subjunctive 'you give').
segments.s629-g48: که introduces the content/purpose clause 'that / so that'. بی‌تو 'without you' is an adverbial phrase. مبادا is an optative/negative wish 'may it not be / let it not happen'. بهی و مهی are two linked epithets — بهی 'good/excellent' and مهی 'moon‑like / beautiful' — forming the objects of the wish (i.e. 'may there be, without you, neither goodness nor moon‑like beauty').
segments.s629-g57: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. The phrase شمشیر خواهد places the noun 'sword' before the verb خواهد (here a classical/future-like auxiliary meaning 'goes/will go' or 'is set to'), and به رزم اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'into the battle' (به = toward/into + رزم = battle + اندرون = inside).
segments.s629-g58: بیابان شود is a subject–verb clause 'the plain/field becomes' (شود = 'becomes'). همچو introduces a simile 'like', so همچو دریای خون = 'like a sea of blood' (دریای is the construct form of دریا, modified by خون).
segments.s629-g59: به بخشش is an adverbial phrase 'in/with regard to generosity' (به used as 'by/with'). چو ابری بود دربار is a simile: چو = 'like', ابری = 'a cloud', بود = past copula 'was', دربار = 'the court' — literally 'with generosity the court was like a cloud.'
segments.s629-g60: بود پیش او is 'was before him / in his presence' (پیش او = 'before him'). گنج دینار خوار is a noun phrase where خوار qualifies the treasure/dinars as worthless or of little value; word order places the predicate after the locative phrase.
segments.s629-g61: پیامی (indefinite object) + رسانم (1st person singular verb) yields 'I send / I will send a message'. سوی شاه هند is the directional phrase 'to the king of India' (سوی = 'to/toward', شاه هند = 'king of Hind/India').
segments.s629-g62: همان is a demonstrative 'the same'; پهلوی qualifies نامه‌ای as 'a Pahlavi (i.e. written in Pahlavi) letter'. برپرند is a verbal form meaning 'they make it fly / dispatch it by flight' (3rd-person plural with بر- + پرند used poetically for sending by winged messengers). The subject is omitted but understood (couriers/messengers).
segments.s629-g49: This is a compact, slightly inverted clause: بدو (to him) is the indirect object placed before the verb; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said' (poetic placement after the object); شنگل is the subject/name that follows the verb (typical older/poetic word order). که introduces the reported speech (a subordinate clause). بر گوی هین is an address or injunction inside that reported clause: بر (on/upon) + گوی (sphere/orb) with the interjection هین (an imperative-like interjection 'halt/stop') placed after it for force.
segments.s629-g50: This is the remainder of the reported clause introduced by که. گوینده is a noun 'the one who speaks / the speaker'; یابد is a present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'may obtain' or 'attain' (classical/poetic present-subjunctive sense); ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; چرخ is 'the sky/heaven'; آفرین here is a noun 'acclamation/praise'. The whole unit expresses purpose/result: 'that the speaker may obtain praise from heaven.'
segments.s629-g51: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'he said'—the past verb again used poetically). کز is the archaic/poetic compound 'که + از' meaning 'who/from whom' (here indicating descent), followed by شاه خسرونژاد 'of the king Khosrow's lineage' (a nominal compound). The phrase acts as a relative specification introduced into reported speech.
segments.s629-g52: که again introduces a relative/subordinate clause; چون here functions temporally 'as/when'; او 'he' is the subject; به گیتی uses به + noun for motion/entry 'into the world' (به = 'towards/into', گیتی = 'world'); ز مادر نزاد is an older passive/preterite construction 'was born from his mother' — نزاد is the classical past of زاییدن placed at the end (verb-final order common in Ferdowsi).
segments.s629-g53: مهست is a poetic predicative form (مَه است) meaning 'is the moon' or 'is moonlike' used before the noun phrase for emphasis; آن سرافراز is the demonstrative + epithet 'that proud/lofty one' serving as logical subject; بر روی دهر is a prepositional phrase 'on the face of the age/world' (بر = on/upon, روی = face, دهر = age/world). The word order (predicate before subject) is an elevated, poetic inversion.
segments.s629-g54: که introduces the explanatory/result clause. با داد او is an instrumental phrase 'by/with his justice' (با = with, داد = the noun 'justice'); زهر شد is verb phrase 'became/was (something) with respect to poison' where شد is the past of شدن 'became'; پای زهر is a noun phrase 'the foot/limit of poison' or idiomatically 'poison's power/stance' (پای = foot/base, زهر = poison). Grammatically, the clause links his justice as the cause affecting 'poison' (classical/poetic usages and inversions present).
segments.s629-g55: بزرگان همه is the topical subject 'all the great ones/nobles' (بزرگان = great ones; همه = all). باژ دار is a poetic adjective/compound (باژ‌دار) meaning roughly 'struck with a cry/terror-stricken' or 'raising a cry' — here it predicates a state affecting those great ones. وی‌اند is the archaic/clitic copula 'they are' (وی‌اند = 'they are' in older Persian). The structure is Subject + predicate + copula, with a poetic compound adjective.
segments.s629-g56: به introduces a prepositional/benefactive relation 'as/to/regarding'; نخچیر شیران is a genitive/possessive grouping 'the quarry/prey of the lions' (نخچیر = prey/spoil, شیران = lions or 'brave men' but here literal 'lions'); شکار وی‌اند is a nominal predicate 'are his prey/quarry' (شکار = prey/quarry, وی‌اند = 'they are'). Overall word order is prepositional phrase + nominal predicate, typical of epic diction.

sentences.s629-s5: His head was lifted aloft; all around there were abundant arms and music.
sentences.s629-s6: Horsemen and elephants all around, at the feet, roared the ringing with trumpets.
sentences.s629-s7: The court‑keeper stood astonished; he set his heart into thought.
sentences.s629-s8: Thus he spoke to his curtain‑keepers, his devotees and steadfast followers.
sentences.s629-s1: When he placed the king's seal upon the messenger, he adorned the hunting-ground with music.
sentences.s629-s2: No one in the army knew of his plan; none accompanied him except his famed men.
sentences.s629-s3: Thus he came to Hindustan and crossed the waters of Jādūstān.
sentences.s629-s4: When he came near Shangal's iwan, he saw its curtains and court.
sentences.s629-s13: His attire was all silver and his body (appeared) gold; he had set jewels here and there.
sentences.s629-s14: An attendant sat close to him; behind him another stood at his feet.
sentences.s629-s15: He saw his brother upon the footstool, a jewel-studded cap placed on his head.
sentences.s629-s16: When he came near the high pavilion, he saw him with a crown on a throne of splendor.
sentences.s629-s17: All the throne's pedestals of gold and crystal — upon them the king sat, with glory and might.
sentences.s629-s18: The king mounted the throne, and they paid him homage; it remained before him for a long time.
sentences.s629-s19: Thus spoke he who is, among kings, like the moon: the sovereign Bahram, God-worshipper.
sentences.s629-s20: I have a certain letter for the king of Hind, a Pahlavi written text on a bird.
sentences.s629-s9: A messenger came from the presence of the victorious King Bahram to this court.
sentences.s629-s10: At the same time the baggage-master went in, bringing the load from behind the curtain up toward the king.
sentences.s629-s11: He commanded; they lifted the curtain, and with honor placed him in the royal presence.
sentences.s629-s12: Proudly striding along, Bahram Gūr saw a certain house whose sky (ceiling) was of crystal.
sentences.s629-s21: When the king heard Bahram's voice, he ordered that a golden low-seat be set.
sentences.s629-s22: They set a golden throne upon it, and they summoned his companions from the court.
sentences.s629-s23: When he sat down, he loosened his lips from their bond and thus said, 'O lofty king'.
sentences.s629-s24: I will open my tongue when you command; may there be, without you, neither beauty nor moon‑like splendor.
sentences.s629-s29: When his sword goes into the fight, the plain becomes like a sea of blood.
sentences.s629-s30: In generosity the court was like a cloud; before him treasure and dinars were of little worth.
sentences.s629-s31: I will send a message to the king of India; the same Pahlavi letter they will dispatch on the wing.
sentences.s629-s25: Shengel said to him, “Halt upon the sphere, so that the speaker may obtain praise from the heavens.”
sentences.s629-s26: Thus he said that he was of Khosrow's royal stock — that when he was born into the world from his mother...
sentences.s629-s27: That lofty one is like the moon upon the face of the age; by his justice poison itself was abated.
sentences.s629-s28: All the great ones are filled with alarm at him; the lions' quarry are his prey.
